ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for yakiniku beef rice bowl; FOODON_00002809 (beef dish) used as the closest defensible parent class. Okra and rice components are not individually coded here.
ontology.foodex2_description: Beef meat dish — mixed preparation, rice-based bowl
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (meat dishes) used as the closest defensible category for a beef yakiniku rice bowl. No exact FoodEx2 term exists for yakiniku-style beef rice bowls with dashi sauce.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No applicable SNOMED CT food concept identified for yakiniku beef rice bowl with okra and dashi sauce.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl serving of approximately 550 g, as listed on the GrabFood menu, containing rice, beef slices, okra, and dashi sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beef, short loin, top loin steak, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/8 inch fat, all grades, cooked, grilled (FDC ID 174033); White Rice, cooked (FDC ID 169756); Okra, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (FDC ID 169260); Soy sauce made from soy and wheat (shoyu) (FDC ID 172238); composite weighted estimate for yakiniku beef rice bowl
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore entry exists for a yakiniku beef rice bowl with okra and dashi sauce. Nutrient values were estimated by compositing USDA FDC entries for cooked beef (grilled), cooked white rice, cooked okra, and soy sauce/dashi components, weighted to approximate a typical yakiniku bowl composition (approximately 45% rice, 30% beef, 10% okra, 15% sauce/condiments by weight). The input record's per-100g values (energy 130.77 kcal, protein 6.92 g, fat 5.38 g, carbohydrate 13.85 g) are broadly consistent with this composite estimate and have been retained with medium confidence. Sodium at 276.9 mg/100g is plausible given soy sauce and dashi content. All values cross-checked against USDA FDC composite logic.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; white rice (Japanese short-grain, cooked) GI ~72 (glucose reference); University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for short-grain white rice
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists in the University of Sydney GI Database specifically for a yakiniku beef rice bowl. The GI of 72 is derived from the dominant carbohydrate component: short-grain Japanese white rice, which has a published GI of approximately 72 (glucose scale) per Atkinson, Foster-Powell & Brand-Miller (2008), Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283, and confirmed in the University of Sydney GI Database. The beef, okra, and dashi sauce components contribute negligible carbohydrates and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (72 × 76.2 g carbohydrate in 550 g serving) / 100 = 54.9, rounded to 54.8.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and dashi stock
hypertension.guidance: At 276.9 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1,523 mg per 550 g bowl), this dish provides over 65% of the WHO-recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg in a single serving; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium soy sauce, less dashi sauce, and avoid adding extra condiments to keep total meal sodium within DASH guideline targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index short-grain white rice with high glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: The bowl's GI of 72 and glycemic load of approximately 54.8 per 550 g serving will cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request a brown rice or mixed-grain rice substitution to lower the GI, reduce portion size, and limit the mirin- and sugar-containing yakiniku sauce per ADA dietary guidelines.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef and moderate sodium load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each 550 g bowl provides approximately 12.7 g saturated fat (2.31 g/100g) and 1,523 mg sodium, both of which exceed single-meal targets for cardiovascular risk management per AHA guidelines; individuals with CVD should choose leaner beef cuts, request less sauce, and pair with a vegetable side to improve the overall dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (beef) is rich in arachidonic acid, amplifying pro-inflammatory omega-6 pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute beef with chicken, tofu, or oily fish to reduce arachidonic acid load; okra and ginger in the dish provide beneficial anti-inflammatory components and should be retained in any substitution.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Okra Beef Yakiniku Bowl with Dashi Sauce (オクラ牛焼肉丼 だしソース添え; Chinese: 秋葵牛肉烧肉饭配出汁酱; Malay: Mangkuk Nasi Daging Lembu Yakiniku dengan Sos Dashi dan Bendi) is a Japanese-style rice bowl dish served in Singapore, featuring thinly sliced beef cooked yakiniku-style — stir-fried or grilled over high heat and then simmered in a savoury dashi-based sauce made from kombu and bonito stock, soy sauce, mirin, sake, and a small amount of sugar. The beef is served over a bed of steamed short-grain Japanese white rice and topped with blanched fresh okra, which adds a mild, slightly mucilaginous texture and a fresh green contrast to the rich, umami-forward beef. The overall flavour profile is savoury, slightly sweet, and deeply umami, with the dashi sauce providing a distinctly Japanese character. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 130.8 kcal, 6.9 g protein, 5.4 g fat (2.3 g saturated), 13.9 g carbohydrate, 1.2 g dietary fibre, and 276.9 mg sodium. The high sodium content (driven by soy sauce and dashi) makes this dish a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The short-grain white rice base gives the dish a high glycemic index of approximately 72 and a glycemic load of approximately 54.8 per 550 g bowl, making it unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes without modification. The saturated fat from beef also warrants caution for cardiovascular disease management. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those following a halal diet without modification, and contains soy and wheat allergens from the soy sauce component.

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for this specific dish. Nutrient values were estimated by compositing USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked beef (grilled), cooked white rice, cooked okra, and soy sauce/dashi components. The input record's per-100g values are broadly consistent with this composite estimate and have been retained. GI is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component (short-grain white rice, GI 72) per University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach and the absence of an exact approved-source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked beef (grilled, FDC 174033), cooked white rice (FDC 169756), cooked okra (FDC 169260), and soy sauce/shoyu (FDC 172238), weighted to approximate a typical yakiniku bowl composition. The input record's grabfood-sourced per-100g values are broadly consistent with this composite and have been retained at medium confidence. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite yakiniku beef rice bowl preparation are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-17. 3. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on short-grain Japanese white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source, per Atkinson, Foster-Powell & Brand-Miller (2008), Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283, and the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. This dish is of Japanese culinary origin served in Singapore. Per the multilingual matrix, Japanese cuisine requires Japanese script (satisfied: オクラ牛焼肉丼 だしソース添え). Because this dish is sold in Singapore and is accessible to a multilingual Singaporean audience, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi names have also been provided to support multilingual retrieval and satisfy the validator's G6 and Q8 requirements. 5. Two portions are listed in the input (550 g bowl, 650 g set meal); the 550 g bowl is used as the default serving for GL calculation. 6. The ethnic_tags in the input ('Japanese Curry', 'Grilled Chicken Skewers') appear to be erroneous metadata artefacts and have been excluded from the output. 7. trans_fat_g of 0.08 g/100g is retained from the input as a plausible trace value for beef-derived trans fats (vaccenic acid); this is not a confirmed zero.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'.

Iteration 3: Added Chinese simplified (秋葵牛肉烧肉饭配出汁酱), Chinese traditional (秋葵牛肉燒肉飯配出汁醬), Malay (Mangkuk Nasi Daging Lembu Yakiniku dengan Sos Dashi dan Bendi), Tamil (ஓக்ரா மாட்டிறைச்சி யாக்கினிகு கிண்ணம் தாஷி சாஸுடன்), and Hindi (ओकरा बीफ याकिनिकु बाउल दाशी सॉस के साथ) local names to satisfy G6 and Q1/Q8 validator requirements. Added all new local-language strings to flat aliases array and aliases_by_language. Verified aliases consistency self-check passes.
